start with the executive summary from
today's leadoff morning note it's a fun
leadoff morning note given that the jobs
support was as sloppy and and and uh as
it was obviously severely impacted by
the Hurricanes uh and the uh Boeing
strikes so it's not like we learned uh
particularly that much about the US
economy in today's job support so let me
just unpack this uh anyway have still
having a little fun this morning so
today's key macro question is uh is the
bear porn newsletter recession finally
commencing no the answer is no our
answer is uh the ratty October 2024 jobs
report does little to discourage our
conviction in our two plus year old
resilient US economy theme if anything
we now have more conviction because of
the likely bounceback in the November or
December data we continue to pound the
table on the now consensus resilient US
economy theme we authored in September
of 2022 recall that at the time we
forecast that if a recession were to
commence it would not commence until 4q
2023 through 1q 2024 at the earliest
that was a heroic call in the face of
then consensus recession fears that were
broadly maintained until this year
moreover once we arrived in that 4q 2023
to 1 q224 window of weakness our daily
Basi and inference process consistently
confirmed a low probability of a
developing recession in the US economy a
condition that has been maintained since
that fundamental research afforded 42
macro clients the confidence to take
advantage of kiss and Dr Mo's bullish
risk management signals all year we're
really proud of that transitioning to
our 42 macro dashboard as always we wrap
up with the question from our community
this one is regarding uh the bond market
is uh reads has the bond market ever
ignored falling uh interest rates uh
says given the sovereign debt
discussions for turning Etc there has
there been a period of time uh when a
central bank has tried to lower the
interest policy rate to Spur growth but
the bond market doesn't react as
expected or care and Government Bond
yelds continue to rise either inflation
expectations still high lack of bond
buyers buyers are sure if they receive
their principle back but the interest
rate is still lowered and lowered in an
effort to turn the economy around I
don't know if this is a plausible
scenario uh but given what I have uh
been hearing on podcast recently I'm
wondering if that could happen seems
likely I'm just not sure if I'm missing
some mechanism between the central banks
uh fed setting uh policy interest rates
and bond yields so uh the answer to this
question is uh yes unfortunately you uh
are missing this but this is why you
have me here to answer the question you
are missing the mechanism so I'll start
by saying the Central Bank does not
control Bond yelds the Central Bank
hopes to influence bond yields by
setting the price of money at the short
end of the curve really at the the at
the overnight part of the curve uh and
so and what they're essentially trying
to do is influence investor expectation
of the terminal policy rate which
ultimately has a significant influence
over uh the prices of of of maturities
uh of of debt matur debt Securities that
mature over different teners uh with
respect to bond yields obviously the
market itself sets bond yield pricing
how bond yields a price is a Confluence
of three things one it's the expected
growth rate uh in the economy uh which
is usually a some function of of of
people's expectations of trend growth uh
it's the expected inflation rate in the
economy which is usually some investors
uh broad expectation of trend inflation
in the economy and then there's the
expected uh uh uh term premium uh and so
the term premum is the uh premium you
get as an investor for locking your
money up uh over a over some various uh
tener over a duration over specific
duration as opposed to what you would
get mathematically by rolling over
short-term interest rates for that same
uh uh uh time period in theory there
should be positively sled ter premum in
the market you should be getting paid to
take illiquidity risk if you're going to
lock up your money let's say for 10 20
or 30 years uh but currently right now
there's very little term Premia in the
market which is something we focused on
uh in our research here 42 macro Vis V
our investing during a fourth turning
regime analysis which has suddenly
become all the rage you know I think
there's a lot more discussion about the
uh propagate fiscal path that the US is
on which is only going to accelerate no
matter who wins the election spoiler
alert and ultimately our view is that
the monetary Authority's likely response
to that is likely to create a secular B
market uh Contin continued secular B
market which we've been on the right
side of uh in things like stocks uh
crypto uh and gold most notably uh and
other assets as well real estate Etc uh
so uh investors um just getting back to
the question have we seen times where
the bond you would actually Ries and
responds to the FED cutting the policy
rate and the answer is yes we've seen
several of those uh instances uh
historically uh dating back to uh as as
as early as the 1970s we had one in the
80s had instance in the 80s we had an
instance in the early 90s as well uh I
want to say we had maybe an instance uh
in 2015 16 as well so there have been
instances where uh we've seen bond
yields rally in response to the FED
cutting uh the policy rate uh and
ultimately it's a function of the
investor expectations for growth
inflation and ultimately turn Premia
which usually is less of a factor but it
has been a big factor in this backup uh
but usually changing investor
expectations for growth in inflation
Rising causing investors to demand those
Securities less go back to the entire um
braki QE program uh you know that was
you know pretty large at the beginning
of my career which uh began on global
Wall Street back in 2009 well all we
always saw that once the FED started
actually performing the QE buying the
bonds we saw bond yields actually rise
which is counterintuitive But ultimately
what it meant is that investors no
longer wanted to hold the quote unquote
least risky Securities uh and they
wanted to move further out on the risk
spectrum because the the FED through its
quantitative easing program was reducing
the supply of those Securities and and
and and lowering the ex an yield so
ironically the ex an yield started
Rising because people were selling them
to the fed and ultimately buying other
risk assets and that's how QE worked
from a balance sheet substitution
perspective so uh you know I put a pin
on this question it was a great question
hopefully investors are uh uh getting U
